summary: Gruber celebrates Sketch's 10th anniversary post about the value of building truly native Mac applications. He highlights Sketch's articulation of what makes native Mac apps special: following Apple's Human Interface Guidelines, fitting in with the OS, and lowering the learning curve. Gruber endorses the idea that great Mac apps should offer deep customization, familiar Mac conventions, and aesthetic beauty. He coins and refines terminology for this category, moving from his earlier 'Mac-assed Mac apps' toward Panic's more elegant 'Mac-app Mac app' phrasing. The post is a love letter to the craft of native Mac software development.
key_points: Native Mac apps that follow Apple's Human Interface Guidelines offer an intuitive familiarity that lowers the learning curve
The best Mac apps combine deep customization, platform-native conventions, and aesthetic beauty as a unified experience
Gruber searches for the right term to describe these apps, preferring Panic's 'Mac-app Mac app' over his own 'Mac-assed Mac app'
The post implicitly contrasts native Mac apps against Electron and cross-platform alternatives without naming them directly
money_quotes: Sketch hits all the key marks about what best defines a great, truly native Mac app, particularly deep Mac tools for professional work.
Customization that allows you, the user, to shape the tool into something personal, that fits your needs and idiosyncrasies.
Familiarity — the je ne sais quoi of doing things, large and small, the Macintosh way — that makes new (or just new to you) Mac apps easy to get started with and intuitive to explore.
And, well, just being a beautiful work of art unto itself.
Je ne sais quoi means 'a quality that cannot be described or named easily' — it's no surprise it's hard to categorize these apps with a term.
Whatever we call them, they're worth embracing and celebrating, and Sketch sure as hell is one of them.
key_insight: The best Mac apps embody a hard-to-name quality of platform-native craftsmanship — deep customization, OS-level familiarity, and aesthetic beauty — that deserves to be celebrated and defended.
